Exercise 5 NPV Profile: Net Present Value at Different Discount Rates 5.1 Dataset 5.2 Data Entry & Formulas 5.3 Chart Creation 5.4 Chart Formatting 5.5 Interpretation 5.6 Sorting 5.7 Advanced Filter 5.8 Final Output 5.9 Conclusion

Exercise 5 NPV Profile: Net Present Value at Different Discount Rates Learning Outcomes Analyse project cash flows to determine Net Present Value (NPV) Apply financial formulas (NPV) at varying discount rates Determine the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) conceptually using an NPV Profile Create an NPV Profile chart to visualize project viability and sensitivity 5.1 Dataset 5.1.1 Cash Flow Data Year Cash Flow (Rs) Description 0 -20000 Initial Investment 1 8000 Year 1 Inflow 2 7000 Year 2 Inflow 3 6000 Year 3 Inflow 4 5000 Year 4 Inflow 5 4400 Year 5 Inflow 6 2400 Year 6 Inflow 5.1.2 NPV Profile Table Discount Rate NPV (Rs) Status Interpretation 0% 12800.00 Accept Project adds Value 5% 8503.22 Accept Project adds Value 10% 5067.60 Accept Project adds Value 15% 2278.55 Accept Project adds Value 16% 1784.05 Accept Project adds Value 20% -16.72 Reject Project destroys value 25% -1929.06 Reject Project destroys value * IRR ≈ the discount rate where NPV = 0. From the table, IRR falls between 16% and 20%.  Download Excel File Download Financial Data Excel 5.2 Data Entry & Formulas 5.2.1 Structure in Excel Column Description A Discount Rate B NPV (Rs) C Status D Interpretation 5.2.2 Formulas Used Calculation Formula NPV =NPV(rate, B5:B10) + B4 (Add initial investment separately as it occurs at Year 0) Status =IF(B14>0, "Accept", "Reject") Interpretation =IF(C14="Accept", "Project adds Value", "Project destroys value") 5.3 Chart Creation 5.3.1 Recommended Charts Line Chart (NPV Profile) -> Plot Discount Rate on X-axis and NPV on Y-axis to visualize where the line crosses zero (the IRR). 5.3.2 Diagram flowchart TD A[Calculate NPVs] --> B[Insert Line Chart] B --> C[Identify IRR at X-Axis intercept] 5.3.3 Insight The project adds value at discount rates up to approximately 19.9%. The Internal Rate of Return (IRR) is between 16% and 20% (since NPV turns negative at 20%). 5.4 Chart Formatting Title: NPV Profile: Sensitivity to Discount Rates X-axis: Discount Rate (%) Y-axis: Net Present Value (Rs) Legend: NPV 5.5 Interpretation The NPV profile illustrates the inverse relationship between the discount rate and the project’s Net Present Value. As the required rate of return (cost of capital) increases, the NPV steadily declines. The project is financially viable (NPV > 0) for discount rates up to roughly 19.9%. At a 20% discount rate, the NPV turns negative (-16.72 Rs), indicating that the project’s exact Internal Rate of Return (IRR) is just under 20%. 5.6 Sorting 5.6.1 Example Sort by NPV (Descending) -> Observe the highest potential value creation at lower discount rates. 5.7 Advanced Filter 5.7.1 Example Criteria Criteria 1: Status Accept OR Criteria 2: NPV (Rs) > 5000 5.8 Final Output Cash flow tabulation and NPV computation across multiple rates Logical IF formulation for investment Accept/Reject status NPV Profile Line Chart visualizing the IRR intercept Capital budgeting decision insights 5.9 Conclusion This experiment demonstrates how to construct an NPV Profile using Excel’s financial functions. By evaluating NPV across various discount rates, decision-makers can determine the project’s IRR visually and assess its sensitivity to changes in the cost of capital.
